citation: Clements, E., Ehrmann, F., Clark, A., Jones, M., McCall, A., & Duffield, R. (2023). Flight path and scheduling effects on perceived jet lag, fatigue, and sleep in footballers traveling to and from national teams. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. https://doi.org/10.1123/ijspp.2023-0027
abstract: This study examined posttravel perceptual responses of national-team footballers (soccer) following different flight paths, arrival/departure times, and trip contexts. Details of 396 flights from 68 national-team players were obtained. Each player provided ratings of perceptual fatigue, sleep, soreness, stress, and jet lag for 2 days before and after each trip. Linear mixed models compared the pretravel with posttravel change in perceptual responses based on flight path, context, and schedule. Perceived jet-lag ratings were more responsive to travel variables (R2 = .48) than other perceptual ratings (R2 < .26). Asia-to-Europe and Europe-to-Australia travel produced the highest jet-lag ratings. Fatigue and sleep scores were worst following Europe-to-Australia travel. Return travel from national team to club produced poorer perceptual responses than all other contexts. Daytime arrivals (11 AM–5 PM) resulted in better perceptual outcomes than early-morning or late-night arrivals.
tldr: Perceived jet-lag ratings are more sensitive to travel demands than general wellness scales in national-team footballers. Europe-to-Australia and Asia-to-Europe routes are most disruptive. Returning to clubs after national team duties is harder on players than traveling to camp. Scheduling arrivals during daytime (11 AM–5 PM) yields the best perceptual recovery outcomes.
methods: Observational longitudinal study using 396 verified flights from 68 senior male national-team soccer players (March 2018–July 2022). Players completed daily perceptual questionnaires (7-point Likert scales for fatigue, sleep, soreness, stress; 10-point jet-lag scale) for 2 days pre- and up to 3 days post-travel. Flight paths were categorized by departure/arrival continent, trip context (outbound, transition, return), and arrival/departure time windows. Linear mixed models with player as random effect were built stepwise; post hoc pairwise comparisons used estimated marginal means.
findings: Jet-lag scores were most sensitive to travel variables (R2 = .48). Asia-to-Europe (p < .05) and Europe-to-Australia (p < .001) produced highest jet-lag ratings. Fatigue was worst after Asia-to-Europe and Europe-to-Australia. Sleep was worst after Europe-to-Australia. Return travel (national team to club) produced poorer total wellness, fatigue, sleep, and soreness than outbound or transition travel (p < .05). Daytime arrivals yielded significantly lower total wellness, fatigue, sleep, and soreness than morning arrivals; night arrivals produced highest stress scores. Older players reported higher jet lag; more experienced players reported lower jet lag.
limitations: Limited control over athlete behavior before, during, or after travel. Low R2 values for wellness scales suggest external factors beyond travel affect scores. Pretravel baseline used a single-day measure, increasing susceptibility to noise. Perceived jet-lag ratings collected at a single daily time point. Insufficient Australia-to-Europe trip data due to match scheduling and COVID-related venue changes.
practicalImplications: Use jet-lag ratings (not just wellness scales) to monitor national-team travel stress. Prioritize travel management interventions for Europe-to-Australia and Asia-to-Europe routes. Provide additional recovery support when athletes return to clubs post-camp. Where logistically possible, schedule arrivals between 11 AM and 5 PM. Develop individualized travel strategies for younger or less-experienced players who show greater jet-lag vulnerability.
athleteDev: Travel load accumulation during multi-trip national team windows can significantly impair perceived recovery. Development of travel literacy and routine (sleep hygiene, light exposure, nutrition timing) in younger national-team players may reduce jet-lag susceptibility over time.
rtp: Return travel after national-team camp represents a distinct recovery challenge beyond the match itself. Practitioners managing athletes returning from international duty should factor in travel-induced fatigue layered on top of match load when planning RTP timelines and training loads in the days following return.

citation: Park, G., & Kent, R. (2020). Foot shape analysis of professional American Football players. Footwear Science. https://doi.org/10.1080/19424280.2020.1769203
abstract: This study quantified the foot shape of NFL players to identify characteristics that inform position-specific footwear design. Statistical shape analysis (SSA) was performed on 3D scans of 290 NFL players' feet from 4 teams during preseason training camps. Eight foot shape measurements were defined to reflect the principal components (PCs) of shape variability. The first three PCs explained 82.3% of foot shape variation: foot volume/size (34.4%), medial longitudinal arch shape with foot length (26.2%), and foot curvature (21.7%). Mean foot length was 293 ± 13.7 mm; width 110.6 ± 6.8 mm. Significant between-position differences were observed primarily in size-related measures. Flat foot prevalence (zero arch depth) was higher in linemen and hybrid groups than skill players.
tldr: NFL players' feet vary most in overall size, arch shape, and curvature—factors that conventional Brannock devices do not fully capture. Linemen and hybrid players are larger in all dimensions and more likely to have flat feet than skill players. This data should inform position-specific last design and footwear selection to optimize fit, performance, and injury prevention at the elite level.
methods: A subset of 290 left-foot 3D scans (from 1,451 total scanned across all 32 NFL teams) was extracted from 4 teams during preseason training camps. Scans were collected with a laser foot scanner (INFOOT USB IFU-S-01) at 1 mm resolution, processed in Meshmixer v3.5. Correlation-based PCA was performed on 240 pseudo-landmarks from 8 transverse cross-sectional contours. Eight foot measurements (foot length, width, arch length, heel width, mid-foot width, ball girth, arch depth, flare angle) were defined and compared across three position groups (linemen, n = 83; hybrid, n = 68; skill, n = 135) using statistical comparisons.
findings: First three PCs explained 82.3% of shape variation: PC1 = foot volume/size (34.4%), PC2 = arch shape with foot length (26.2%), PC3 = foot curvature (21.7%). All measurements except arch depth and flare angle differed significantly between position groups (p < .05). Linemen had the largest feet overall (length 303.7 mm, width 117.0 mm, ball girth 277.0 mm). Flat foot prevalence (arch depth = 0) was greater in linemen and hybrid groups than skill players. Longer feet associated with lower arch height, consistent with prior literature.
limitations: Only left-foot scans were analyzed. Sample was a subset of 4 teams from the full 32-team database, though weight and height distributions were comparable to the full NFL population. Kickers and punters were excluded due to insufficient scan numbers. Scans captured static unloaded foot shape; dynamic loading during play may alter effective foot dimensions.
practicalImplications: NFL footwear design should be position-specific, accounting for differences in foot size and arch shape between linemen, hybrid, and skill players. Flat-foot prevalence in linemen and hybrid players supports dedicated arch support and last modifications for those groups. The 8 measurements identified here provide a practical framework for footwear fitting systems beyond the traditional Brannock device.
athleteDev: Understanding position-specific foot morphology in American football players can guide footwear selection during player development pipelines. Proper footwear fit improves comfort, performance, and reduces injury risk from conditions such as fifth metatarsal fractures, turf toe, Lisfranc injuries, and blisters, which are relevant across developmental and elite levels.
rtp: Foot shape data can inform orthotics and footwear modifications for athletes returning from foot and ankle injuries. Position-specific arch and curvature profiles should be considered when prescribing return-to-play footwear to ensure mechanical support is appropriate for the demands of the player's position.

citation: Cosio, P. L., Moreno-Simonet, L., Fernández, D., Lloret, M., Padulles, X., Padulles, J. M., Farran-Codina, A., Rodas, G., & Cadefau, J. A. (2024). Football (soccer) match-derived hamstring muscles residual fatigue can be monitored using early rate of torque development. European Journal of Applied Physiology. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00421-024-05694-x
abstract: This study investigated whether a soccer match affects the rapid force-generating capacity of the hamstring muscles using rate of torque development (RTD) and serum biomarkers. Nineteen male, regional first-division soccer players (age 20.9 ± 2.0 years) were assessed before and at 24, 48, and 72 hours post-match. Hamstring MVIC torque and RTD were measured via the 90°hip:20°knee isometric posterior chain (IPC) test. Serum biomarkers included CK, sMtCK, transaminases, MDA, and irisin. Early RTD (0–50 ms, 0–100 ms) remained significantly reduced at MD+3 in both dominant and non-dominant hamstrings (p < 0.001, g = −1.24 and −1.06). MVIC torque was unrecovered at MD+2 but recovered at MD+3. Neuromuscular deficits were coupled with elevated myocyte structural and mitochondrial damage biomarkers persisting through MD+3.
tldr: Early rate of torque development (RTD at 0–50 ms and 0–100 ms) is a more sensitive indicator of hamstring residual fatigue than MVIC torque alone after a soccer match. Hamstring rapid force-generating capacity is not fully recovered within 3 days post-match. The 90°hip:20°knee IPC test provides a practical, portable, cost-effective tool for monitoring hamstring recovery during congested schedules, and findings support the IOC recommendation of at least 96 hours between matches to allow adequate recovery.
methods: Single-group repeated measures design in 19 male regional first-division soccer players. Neuromuscular assessments (MVIC torque and RTD at 0–50, 0–100, 0–250 ms and peak RTD) were performed using the 90°hip:20°knee IPC test with a custom rigid strain gauge before the match (MD), and at MD+1 (24h), MD+2 (48h), and MD+3 (72h). Match load was monitored via 10-Hz IMU devices. Serum biomarkers (CK, CK/ALT, CK/AST, sMtCK, MDA, irisin) were collected at each time point. Statistical analysis included two-way repeated measures ANOVA for neuromuscular data and one-way ANOVA for biomarkers, with post hoc Bonferroni-corrected paired t-tests.
findings: Early RTD0–50 and RTD0–100 remained significantly reduced at MD+3 (p < 0.001, g = −1.24 and −1.06) in both dominant and non-dominant hamstrings. MVIC torque was unrecovered at MD+2 (p = 0.004, g = −0.91; p = 0.002, g = −0.98) but recovered by MD+3. RTD0–250 and RTDpeak did not change significantly. Normalized RTD (expressed as % MVIC torque/s) also showed significant decreases at 0–50 and 0–100 ms intervals through MD+3. CK peaked at MD+1 (+499%), remained elevated through MD+3 (+124%). sMtCK, CK/ALT, CK/AST, MDA, and irisin were all elevated through MD+3, confirming sustained structural and oxidative muscle damage.
limitations: The strain gauge had a low baseline noise amplitude and lacked 1000 Hz sampling frequency as recommended by Maffiuletti et al. (2016); however, recent evidence shows this does not substantially affect reliability at the frequencies used. Small sample size (n = 19) in a single competitive standard (regional first division). Monitoring was limited to 72 hours; full recovery timeline beyond 72 hours was not captured. Individual nutritional intake between meals was not fully controlled despite dietary standardization around the match.
practicalImplications: Use the 90°hip:20°knee IPC test and early RTD metrics (0–50 ms, 0–100 ms) as a non-exhaustive, cost-effective, portable tool to track hamstring recovery in soccer players during congested schedules. Early RTD deficits persist beyond MVIC recovery and represent a distinct, clinically relevant neuromuscular target. Practitioners should not rely on MVIC torque alone when clearing players from post-match hamstring fatigue, particularly given the risk of hamstring strain injury during sprinting with impaired rapid force production.
athleteDev: Monitoring hamstring rapid force-generating capacity through RTD is important for long-term athlete management in soccer. Developing hamstring strengthening programs that target rapid force production (e.g., flywheel, sprint-specific eccentric work) may help buffer post-match RTD decrements and reduce hamstring strain injury incidence across high-volume developmental and professional schedules.
rtp: Early RTD at 0–50 ms and 0–100 ms should be incorporated into hamstring RTP criteria following hamstring strain injury, as these metrics are more sensitive indicators of residual neuromuscular deficit than peak torque alone. Players returning to full match participation within 72 hours of a prior match face elevated hamstring injury risk due to unresolved rapid force-generating deficits and persistent biochemical markers of muscle damage.

citation: Schuth, G., Szigeti, G., Dobreff, G., Pašić, A., Gabbett, T., Szilas, A., & Pavlik, G. (2024). Football movement profile-based creatine kinase prediction performs similarly to GPS derived machine learning models in National team soccer players. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. https://doi.org/10.1123/ijspp.2024-0077
abstract: This study compared GPS/IMA-derived and Football Movement Profile (FMP)-derived machine learning models for predicting next-day creatine kinase (CK) in national-team soccer players, and compared general versus individualized prediction models. A total of 444 national-team male soccer players (U15 to senior) were monitored during 2019–2022 with 10-Hz GPS units, and morning CK was measured from whole blood, yielding 8,570 data points. Machine learning models were built using GPS+IMA, FMP, or combined parameters. The FMP model (R2 = .60, RMSE = 144.6 U/L) performed similarly to GPS (R2 = .62, RMSE = 141.2 U/L) and the combination (R2 = .62, RMSE = 140.3 U/L). General models outperformed individualized models (R2 = .57 vs .37 on average).
tldr: Six FMP parameters (standing, walking, medium running, high running, medium dynamic, high dynamic durations) can predict next-day CK with accuracy comparable to GPS and inertial sensor-derived machine learning models in national-team soccer players. Because FMP is based on inertial sensors rather than GPS satellites, it works indoors and in large stadiums and allows live monitoring during training and matches. General population-level models outperform individualized models when large datasets are available.
methods: Observational study (2019–2022) in 444 elite male national-team soccer players (U15 to senior; age 19.2 ± 4.7 years). GPS (10 Hz, Catapult S7/G7) and capillary blood CK (morning fasted, Reflotron Plus and Tascom SimplexTas 101) were collected across 4 training camp schedule types. Machine learning models (linear regression, ridge, lasso, gradient boosting, random forest) were built using GPS+IMA, FMP, or combined parameters to predict absolute next-day CK. Walk-forward cross-validation (6-fold, time-ordered) was used. Individualized models were built for 161 players with ≥20 data points. SHAP values characterized feature importance.
findings: FMP-only model: R2 = .60, MAE = 88.2 U/L, RMSE = 144.6 U/L, MAPE = 0.346. GPS-only: R2 = .62, MAE = 85.6 U/L, RMSE = 141.1 U/L. GPS+FMP combined: R2 = .62, MAE = 85.3 U/L, RMSE = 140.3 U/L. Differences between the three input sets were negligible. Actual CK was the top predictor (SHAP). FMP dynamic duration metrics (medium-high, high) were the most important FMP features. General models outperformed individualized models in 73% of cases (R2 .57–.62 vs .37). Players with >70 data points showed improved individual model performance but still did not surpass general model accuracy.
limitations: Wide age range (14–33 years) may introduce biological maturity confounds, though most U15 players were post-peak height velocity. CK measurement systems changed mid-study; a correction equation was developed and validated. Individual nutrition between standardized meals could not be fully controlled. Recovery protocols (ice bath, contrast bath) were standardized but may have influenced CK kinetics. Time between last session and morning CK draw could not be precisely controlled.
practicalImplications: Practitioners can use FMP-based monitoring (6 simple categories) as a practical alternative to GPS for predicting next-day muscle damage when GPS is unavailable (indoors, large stadiums). Live FMP monitoring enables immediate feedback to coaches, allowing real-time load adjustments. General population models with large datasets are more reliable than individualized models for most players, though individualized models may be worth pursuing if >70 matched data points per player are available.
athleteDev: FMP-based CK prediction can assist national team staff in managing training load during congested camp schedules across all age groups (U15 to senior). Identifying players at elevated CK risk based on their movement profile enables proactive periodization and reduced injury exposure during developmental national team programs.
rtp: CK prediction models can inform post-match recovery protocols and return-to-train decisions in congested national team schedules. By predicting next-day muscle damage from match or training load data, staff can identify players who may not tolerate full training intensity on subsequent days, supporting structured RTP progressions following muscle damage events.

citation: Ashley, C. D., Lopez, R. M., & Tritsch, A. J. (2023). Football practices in hot environments impact subsequent days' hydration. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. https://doi.org/10.1519/JSC.0000000000004600
abstract: This study examined the accuracy of urinary hydration measures and carryover effects of consecutive outdoor football practices in hot conditions (mean WBGT = 31.3 ± 1.8°C) in 31 male high school football players (age 16 ± 1 years) across 7 outdoor practices over 2 preseason weeks. Urine color (Ucol) and urine-specific gravity (USG) were compared against percent body mass loss (%BML) using ROC analysis. Neither Ucol nor USG was a valid standalone predictor of ≥2% BML (AUC = 0.55 and 0.49; sensitivity/specificity both ~60%/46%). However, Ucol accurately identified USG > 1.020 (AUC = 0.85). Significant carryover effects of Ucol were found between practices with less than 24 hours recovery, including 2-a-day sessions. %BML was significantly greater after 2-a-day practices.
tldr: Back-to-back and twice-daily football practices in hot conditions produce cumulative hydration deficits measurable via urine color carryover. Neither urine color nor urine-specific gravity alone reliably detects ≥2% body mass loss in this population, though urine color effectively tracks USG. Athletes must actively replenish fluids during and between sessions—especially during 2-a-day preseason structures—to maintain euhydration and reduce exertional heat illness risk.
methods: Observational field study in 31 male high school football players during the first 2 weeks of preseason (7 outdoor practices analyzed; mean WBGT 31.3 ± 1.8°C). Pre- and post-practice urine samples were collected for Ucol (8-color Armstrong scale) and USG (refractometry). Seminude body mass was measured pre and post each practice to calculate %BML. ROC curve analysis assessed Ucol and USG sensitivity/specificity for detecting ≥2% BML. Paired-samples t-tests assessed postpractice to next prepractice carryover. Repeated-measures ANOVA assessed carryover trends across all practices.
findings: ROC analysis: AUC for Ucol to detect ≥2% BML = 0.55 (p = 0.53), USG AUC = 0.49 (p = 0.90); neither was significant. Sensitivity for both = 60%; specificity = 48.8% (Ucol) and 44% (USG). Ucol significantly detected USG > 1.020 (AUC = 0.85, sensitivity 82.5%, specificity 67.9%). Significant Ucol carryover effects between practices with <24 hours recovery (practices 2→3, 6→7AM, 7AM→7PM, 10→11; p ≤ 0.004; effect sizes 0.65–1.10). Significant USG carryover only from practice 2 to 3 (p = 0.011, ES = 0.51). No significant body mass carryover. %BML significantly greater after 2-a-day practices (7PM practice: 1.48 ± 0.75%; practice 10: 1.32 ± 0.72%) versus early practices and baseline (~0.59–0.82%).
limitations: Convenience sample from one high school varsity team limits generalizability. Small sample size (n = 31) may have reduced power to detect smaller carryover effects. Mixed indoor/outdoor practice schedule meant only 7 of 11 practices were analyzed. %BML rarely exceeded 2% in this sample, limiting the utility of testing urinary measures against that threshold. Individual variations in dietary and fluid intake between practices could not be fully controlled.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and strength coaches managing preseason American football in hot environments should track urine color trends across consecutive practice days rather than relying on a single snapshot. Fluid replenishment protocols must be reinforced especially after 2-a-day practices and when less than 24 hours separates sessions. Urine color can serve as a practical proxy for USG in field settings without refractometer access. Cumulative hydration deficits across a preseason week are a tangible exertional heat illness risk factor that requires proactive management.
athleteDev: High school and developing football players practicing in hot environments accumulate hydration deficits across back-to-back sessions. Educating young athletes on consistent fluid intake routines—before, during, and after each practice—is a key component of heat illness prevention during preseason development periods. Equipment additions (pads, helmets) added progressively across preseason further increase sweat loss and compound hydration challenges.
rtp: Athletes returning to full-contact or full-load practices after a heat-related illness or in hot preseason environments should be monitored with serial urinary hydration markers (Ucol, USG) and body mass checks across consecutive days. Residual hypohydration from previous practices can impair thermoregulation, increasing risk during RTP progressions in hot conditions; ensuring euhydration before each session is a prerequisite for safe return.
